title: CS336: Language Modeling from Scratch
description: A hands-on Stanford course that teaches you to build a language model entirely from scratch — covering data collection and cleaning, transformer construction, training, and evaluation before deployment.
longDescription: Language models are the cornerstone of modern NLP and enable a single general-purpose system to address a wide range of downstream tasks. Inspired by operating-systems courses that build an entire OS from scratch, CS336 walks students through every aspect of language model creation — data collection and cleaning for pre-training, transformer model construction, model training, and evaluation before deployment. Lecture videos are available for Spring 2026 and Spring 2025.
prerequisite: Proficiency in Python and PyTorch, a solid deep learning background, and some systems experience

title: CME 296: Diffusion & Large Vision Models
description: A Stanford course on diffusion-based generative models for vision — the foundations of diffusion, score matching and flow matching, modern architectures like Diffusion Transformers, and methods for controllable image generation and evaluation.
longDescription: This course explores diffusion-based generative models for vision. You will study the foundations of diffusion, score matching and flow matching, modern architectures such as U-Nets and Diffusion Transformers, and methods for controllable image generation and evaluation. The course combines theory with practical insights into state-of-the-art generative models. Ideal for students with a background in linear algebra, probability, calculus, and machine learning.
prerequisite: Linear algebra, probability, calculus, and machine learning

title: CME 295: Transformers & Large Language Models
description: A Stanford course on Transformers and Large Language Models — the evolution of NLP methods, the core components of the Transformer architecture and how they power LLMs, and techniques to enhance model performance for real-world applications.
longDescription: This course explores the world of Transformers and Large Language Models (LLMs). You will learn the evolution of NLP methods, the core components of the Transformer architecture, along with how they relate to LLMs as well as techniques to enhance model performance for real-world applications. Through a mix of theory and practical insights, this course will equip you with the knowledge to leverage LLMs effectively.
prerequisite: Machine learning and neural network basics

title: CS153: Frontier Systems
description: A Stanford seminar that puts you in the room with the global leaders solving the biggest bottlenecks in frontier technology — spanning energy, silicon, models, applications, security, and deployment policy across the infrastructure stack.
longDescription: From energy, silicon and models to applications, security and deployment policy, the infrastructure stack is undergoing a generational rewrite. Each week, this course puts you in the room with the global leaders solving the biggest bottlenecks on frontier technology progress. The Spring 2026 lineup features speakers including Sam Altman, Jensen Huang, Ben Horowitz, and others from across the frontier AI ecosystem.
prerequisite: Interest in AI systems and infrastructure

title: CS25: Transformers United V6
description: One of Stanford's hottest seminar courses on Transformers, featuring top researchers — from Geoffrey Hinton and Ashish Vaswani to Andrej Karpathy — covering the latest breakthroughs across LLMs, art, biology, and robotics.
longDescription: CS25 has become one of Stanford's hottest seminar courses, featuring top researchers at the forefront of Transformers research such as Geoffrey Hinton, Ashish Vaswani, and Andrej Karpathy. Each week, the class dives into the latest breakthroughs in AI, from large language models like GPT to applications in art, biology, and robotics. Now on its sixth iteration, the course brings fresh perspectives on where Transformer research is heading next. Lectures are open to everyone — you can audit in person or join the Zoom livestreams without signing up or being affiliated with Stanford.
prerequisite: Basic deep learning background

title: CS224R: Deep Reinforcement Learning
description: A Stanford course on algorithms for deep reinforcement learning — learning behavior from experience using deep neural networks, with a practical focus and applications in robotics, control, and language modeling.
longDescription: Humans, animals, and robots must make decisions and take actions in the world, and it is essential to consider the consequences of those decisions. This course is about algorithms for deep reinforcement learning, with a focus on practical methods that use deep neural networks to learn behavior from high-dimensional observations. Topics include learning from demonstrations, model-based and model-free deep RL, learning from offline datasets, and advanced techniques such as goal-conditioned RL, meta-RL, and unsupervised skill discovery — instantiated with examples from robotics, visual navigation, and control. Complementary to CS234, this course takes a more applied, deep-learning focus with an emphasis on robotics and language modeling.
prerequisite: Machine learning, deep learning, probability, and Python (CS229 / CS230 level)

title: AI Engineering from Scratch
description: A free, open-source curriculum that builds every algorithm from raw math before importing a single framework — 503 lessons across 20 phases, spanning linear algebra to autonomous agent swarms, in Python, TypeScript, Rust, and Julia.
longDescription: This curriculum is the spine: 20 phases, 503 lessons, and four languages (Python, TypeScript, Rust, and Julia), running from linear algebra at one end to autonomous swarms at the other. Every algorithm is built from raw math first — backpropagation, tokenizer, attention, the agent loop — so that by the time PyTorch shows up, you already understand what it is doing under the hood. Each lesson runs the same loop: read the problem, derive the math, write the code, run the test, keep the artifact. No five-minute videos, no copy-paste deploys, no hand-holding — free, open source (MIT), and built to run on your own laptop. Maintained by Rohit Ghumare and contributors.
prerequisite: Basic programming

title: CS231N: Deep Learning for Computer Vision
description: Stanford's flagship deep learning for computer vision course — a deep dive into end-to-end neural networks for core vision tasks, alongside modern transformers, diffusion models, and vision-language models.
longDescription: Computer vision is ubiquitous, powering search, image understanding, mapping, medicine, drones, and self-driving cars. This course is a deep dive into deep learning methods with a focus on end-to-end models for core vision tasks such as image classification, localization, and detection — alongside modern approaches like transformers, diffusion models, and vision-language models that power today's AI systems. Over the 10-week course, students implement and train their own neural networks, gain a detailed understanding of cutting-edge research, and tackle real-world vision problems through hands-on assignments and a final project, learning practical engineering tricks for training and fine-tuning deep networks.
instructor: Fei-Fei Li, Ehsan Adeli, and others
prerequisite: Python, calculus, linear algebra, and basic machine learning

title: LLMs for General Audience
description: Andrej Karpathy's series provides a comprehensive introduction to Large Language Models, covering their development, capabilities, and practical applications in an accessible manner.
longDescription: Andrej Karpathy's YouTube series for a general audience focuses on explaining Large Language Models (LLMs) in an accessible way. His comprehensive videos cover topics such as the training process of LLMs, tokenization, neural network internals, and practical applications. He also discusses the limitations and future directions of these models, including the need for advanced reinforcement learning techniques. The content is designed to empower viewers to understand and effectively use the latest AI technologies.
prerequisite: LLMs, General AI knowledge, Generative AI

title: Advanced NLP
description: This course covers advanced NLP concepts using neural networks.
longDescription: The course provides a comprehensive overview of modern NLP methods, focusing on neural networks and their applications. It covers topics such as syntactic, semantic, and discourse analysis, retrieval-augmented generation, and fairness in NLP models. The course culminates in a project where students reimplement and improve upon a research paper.
prerequisite: Python, Probability, Linear Algebra, and basic neural networks

title: Natural Language Processing with Deep Learning
description: This course introduces the fundamental concepts of NLP with deep learning methods.
longDescription: This course provides a thorough introduction to cutting-edge research in deep learning applied to NLP. The course focuses on neural networks, which are a class of deep learning models that have recently obtained improvements in many different NLP tasks.
prerequisite: Python, Deep Learning basics

title: Neural Networks: Zero to Hero
description: This course covers the fundamentals of neural networks, starting from scratch and progressing to advanced models like GPT.
longDescription: The course begins with the basics of backpropagation and builds up to multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs), convolutional neural networks (CNNs), and modern deep neural networks such as generative pre-trained transformers (GPTs). It also covers diagnostic tools for understanding neural network dynamics and performance, as well as tokenization and byte-pair encoding (BPE). The focus is on language models, but the skills learned are transferable to other areas like computer vision.
prerequisite: Python, basic math (e.g., derivatives, Gaussian)

title: Deep Learning for Computer Vision
description: This course covers neural-network-based deep learning methods for computer vision.
longDescription: The course provides a deep dive into neural networks for visual recognition tasks such as image classification and object detection. Students learn to implement, train, and debug their own neural networks and gain a detailed understanding of cutting-edge research in computer vision. Topics include learning algorithms, neural network architectures, and practical engineering tricks for training and fine-tuning networks.
prerequisite: Python, basic linear algebra, and calculus

title: Multimodal Machine Learning
description: This course covers fundamental mathematical concepts in multimodal machine learning, including representation, alignment, and fusion across different modalities.
longDescription: The course addresses the challenges of integrating multiple communicative modalities such as linguistic, acoustic, and visual messages. It focuses on six main challenges: representation, alignment, reasoning, generation, transference, and quantification. Topics include multimodal transformers, neuro-symbolic models, and multimodal tensor fusion. Applications discussed include multimodal affect recognition, image and video captioning, and language-vision navigation.
prerequisite: Python, basic machine learning and deep learning

title: Introduction to Deep Learning
description: This course provides an introduction to deep learning techniques, focusing on neural networks and their applications in language technologies.
longDescription: The course covers the fundamentals of neural networks, including feedforward, convolutional, and recurrent architectures. It explores how these models are applied in various tasks related to language technologies, such as natural language processing and speech recognition. Students gain hands-on experience with deep learning tools and frameworks.
prerequisite: Basic programming and math

title: Large Language Model Agents MOOC
description: This course provides a comprehensive overview of LLM agents, covering foundational concepts, essential abilities, and real-world applications.
longDescription: The course begins with the foundation of large language models (LLMs) and explores their essential abilities for task automation. It delves into LLM agent infrastructure, retrieval-augmented generation, and various applications such as code generation, robotics, web automation, medical applications, and scientific discovery. The course also addresses privacy, safety, ethics, human-agent interaction, and multi-agent collaboration. Industry leaders provide guest lectures, offering insights into the latest developments and challenges in the field.
prerequisite: Basic understanding of AI and programming

title: Neural Networks - 3Blue1Brown
description: This series visually explains the basics of neural networks, including how they learn and process information.
longDescription: The series covers the structure and mathematical description of neural networks, starting with simple neurons and progressing to complex networks. It explores how neural networks learn from data using gradient descent and how they can be applied to tasks like image recognition.
prerequisite: Basic math background

title: The Essence of Linear Algebra - 3Blue1Brown
description: This series provides a visual introduction to linear algebra, focusing on matrices, transformations, and vector spaces.
longDescription: The series covers foundational concepts such as vectors, linear combinations, and matrix transformations. It emphasizes visualizing these concepts to deepen understanding, including topics like determinants, eigenvectors, and abstract vector spaces.
prerequisite: Basic math background

title: The Essence of Calculus - 3Blue1Brown
description: This series visually explains the core ideas of calculus, focusing on derivatives, integrals, and limits.
longDescription: The series aims to make calculus seem discoverable by emphasizing intuitive visual explanations. It covers topics such as the power rule, chain rule, and fundamental theorem of calculus, using geometric and intuitive approaches.
prerequisite: Basic math background

title: Reinforcement Learning
description: This course introduces the foundations of reinforcement learning, covering theoretical concepts and practical applications.
longDescription: The course covers key topics such as Markov decision processes, planning by dynamic programming, model-free prediction and control, value function approximation, policy gradient methods, and deep reinforcement learning. It emphasizes both theoretical understanding and practical implementation of reinforcement learning algorithms in complex environments. The course is designed to equip students with the skills to apply reinforcement learning to real-world problems.
prerequisite: Probability, calculus, linear algebra, and introductory machine learning
